'''Tawera Nuieia Nikau'''<ref>''Lion Red Rugby League Annual 1993'', [[New Zealand Rugby League]], 1993. p.1</ref> {{post-nominals|country=NZL|MNZM|size=85%}} (born 1 January 1967) is a New Zealand former professional [[rugby league]] footballer who played in the 1990s and 2000s.<ref name="Statistics at rugbyleagueproject.org"/> A [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand international]] representative forward, he played club football at a number of different clubs in New Zealand, England and Australia during his career, including the [[Melbourne Storm]]'s victory in the [[1999 NRL season]] Grand Final.

==Early years==
Nikau was born in [[Huntly, New Zealand]]. He began his career playing rugby league for the [[Otara Scorpions]] and [[rugby union]] for the East Tamaki Rugby Union club in [[Auckland]] before his family moved to the [[Waikato District|Waikato]].<ref>''Superleague'', Issue 1 2011. p.22.</ref>

==Playing career==
A [[Waikato rugby league team|Waikato]] and [[Auckland rugby league team|Auckland]] representative, Nikau played his early club football for the [[Otahuhu Leopards]], [[Sheffield Eagles (1984)|Sheffield Eagles]], [[York Wasps]] (1990-1) and [[Castleford Tigers]]. Tawera Nikau played {{rlp|lf}} in [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]]'s 0–17 defeat by [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]] at [[Wembley Stadium (1923)|Wembley Stadium]] on Saturday 16 October 1993.<ref name="Statistics at thecastlefordtigers.co.uk">{{cite web|url=http://www.thecastlefordtigers.co.uk/playerprofile.php?pid=247|title=Statistics at thecastlefordtigers.co.uk|date=31 December 2013|access-date=1 January 2014|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121106235924/http://www.thecastlefordtigers.co.uk/playerprofile.php?pid=247|archive-date=6 November 2012}}</ref> Tawera Nikau played {{rlp|lf}} in [[Castleford Tigers|Castleford]]'s 12–28 defeat by [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]] in the [[1992 Challenge Cup]] Final during the [[1991–92 Rugby Football League season|1991–92 season]] at [[Wembley Stadium (1923)|Wembley Stadium]], [[London]] on Saturday 2 May 1992, in front of a crowd of 77,386.<ref name="2nd May 1992: Wigan 28 Castleford 12 (Challenge Cup Final)">{{cite web|url=http://wigan.rlfans.com/news.php?readmore=3066|title=2nd May 1992: Wigan 28 Castleford 12 (Challenge Cup Final)|publisher=wigan.rlfans.com|date=31 December 2016|access-date=1 January 2017|archive-date=15 February 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170215030604/http://wigan.rlfans.com/news.php?readmore=3066|url-status=dead}}</ref> Tawera Nikau played {{rlp|lf}} in [[Castleford Tigers]]' 33–2 victory over [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]] in the [[1993–94 League Cup (rugby league)|1993–94 Regal Trophy]] Final during the [[1993–94 Rugby Football League season|1993–94 season]] at [[Headingley Rugby Stadium|Headingley]], [[Leeds]] on Saturday 22 January 1994.<ref name="Wigan 2 - 33 Castleford">{{cite web|url=http://www.thecastlefordtigers.co.uk/viewreport.php?page=report&id=775|title=Wigan 2 - 33 Castleford|publisher=thecastlefordtigers.co.uk|date=31 December 2011|access-date=1 January 2012}}</ref> He spent the 1988 season with the [[Canterbury Bulldogs]] on the [[New Zealand Rugby League]]'s "Rookie Scheme".<ref>Lion Red 1989 Rugby League Annual ''New Zealand Rugby League'', p.11</ref> Having already spent several seasons playing in England, during which time he earned representative honours for [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]], from 1996 to 1999, Nikau played in the Australian NRL, spending the 1996–1997 with the [[Cronulla Sharks]], losing the 1997 Super League Grand Final to the Broncos in Brisbane. He joined the [[Melbourne Storm]] team in its 1998 inaugural year, helping the team reach a third-place finish in the minor premiership. Melbourne also did well the following season and Nikau played as a {{rlp|lk}} in the [[1999 NRL Grand Final]] victory over the [[St. George-Illawarra Dragons]]. Nikau's performance has been credited with sparking Melbourne's second-half comeback.<ref>{{cite news 
|last = Cockerill
|first = Ian
|title = Eye of the Storm
|work = [[The Sunday Age]]
|pages = 4
|date = 1999-10-03
|url = http://newsstore.fairfax.com.au/apps/viewDocument.ac?page=1&sy=smh&docID=news991004_0014_7918
|access-date = 2009-10-06}}</ref> From 1999 to 2001, Nikau was under contract with [[Super League]] franchise, the [[Warrington Wolves]].

As a player, he was known for his high energy, fearless charges and his sometimes terrifying facial expressions while playing.  In defense he was often seen to be "everywhere"; making a tackle on one side of the field and the following the ball to complete a tackle on the other.

Nikau suffered a personal tragedy on 5 April 2001, when his wife Letitia died by suicide. After taking some time from rugby to spend with his children in New Zealand, Nikau completed the season. He won in a charity boxing match in June 2002 against Samoan rugby union footballer [[Peter Fatialofa]], supporting the Yellow Ribbon Fight for Life to prevent youth suicide.

==Representative career==
Between 1989 and 1997 Nikau was a [[New Zealand national rugby league team]] representative, although his international career was hindered by a dispute with [[Richie Blackmore (rugby league)|Richie Blackmore]] that resulted in his refusal to play in the same side as Blackmore.

Nikau toured with the [[New Zealand Māori rugby league team|New Zealand Māori side]] in 1996 and 1998 and played at the [[1986 Pacific Cup]].

Nikau attended the [[2000 Rugby League World Cup|2000 World Cup]] as captain of the [[New Zealand Māori rugby league team|Aotearoa Māori side]].<ref name="Māori">''New Zealand Rugby League Annual '98'', [[New Zealand Rugby League]], 1998. p.118</ref><ref name=maori>{{cite book
|last =John Coffey, Bernie Wood
|title = 100 years: Māori rugby league, 1908-2008
|publisher =Huia Publishers
|year = 2008
|pages =  292
|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=nklWo8vw-iIC
|isbn =9781869693312}}</ref>

In 2008, Nikau was part of the Pacific Island Barbarians team at the [[Wheelchair Rugby League World Cup]].<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.rlef.eu.com/news.php?id=955 |title=England Wheelchair team beat Australia in World Cup|date=7 November 2008 |publisher=RLEF |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20091228101806/http://www.rlef.eu.com/news.php?id=955 |archive-date=28 December 2009}}</ref>
 
==Coaching & RL Administration==
In 2004 Nikau was the inaugural coach of the [[Waicoa Bay Stallions]] in the [[Bartercard Cup]]. In 2004 and 2005 he coached the [[New Zealand Māori rugby league team]].

In November 2006, Tawera was appointed New Zealand and Islander Liaison Officer with the [[South Sydney Rabbitohs]].

In 2010 Nikau was appointed a [[New Zealand national rugby league team]] selector for two years.<ref name="NZ_Herald_10633786">{{cite news|url=http://www.nzherald.co.nz/sport/news/article.cfm?c_id=4&objectid=10633786|title=Iro, Nikau named Kiwis selectors|date=23 March 2010|agency=[[NZPA]]|work=[[The New Zealand Herald]]|access-date=19 November 2011}}</ref>  Nikau also completed the [[New York City Marathon]] with [[Frank Bunce]].<ref name="TVNZ_3883878">{{cite news|url=http://tvnz.co.nz/rugby-league-news/nikau-completes-marathon-challenge-3883878|title=Nikau completes marathon challenge|date=8 November 2010|work=[[One Sport]]|access-date=19 November 2011}}</ref>

In 2011 he was added as a consultant to the board of directors of the newly formed USARL.<ref name="USA Rugby League (USARL)">{{cite web|url=http://www.usarugbyleague.com|title=USA Rugby League (USARL)|publisher=usarugbyleague.com|date=18 February 2011|access-date=1 January 2012}}</ref>

==Later years==
<!-- Deleted image removed: [[File:Standing Tall The Tawera Nikau Story.jpg|200px|left]] -->
In 2003 Nikau had a motorcycle accident which resulted in an amputation of his right leg. Nevertheless, he participated in the 2004 Yellow Ribbon Fight for Life, defeating [[Tea Ropati]]. In that same year, his athletic career to date and the details of the tragedies he had survived were described in the biography ''Standing Tall''.

In 2012, Nikau was found guilty of assaulting his estranged daughter Heaven-Leigh outside the [[Huntly, New Zealand|Huntly]] police station,<ref name="'Step up' demands league star's daughter">{{cite web|url=http://www.nzherald.co.nz/nz/news/article.cfm?c_id=1&objectid=10810328|work=The New Zealand Herald|title='Step up' demands league star's daughter|date=3 June 2012}}</ref> he was convicted and fined after he failed to get a discharge without conviction.<ref name="League star Nikau fined over assault on daughter">{{cite web|url=http://www.stuff.co.nz/national/crime/7245541/League-star-Nikau-fined-over-assault-on-daughter|title=League star Nikau fined over assault on daughter|publisher=stuff.co.nz|date=9 July 2012|accessdate=1 January 2013}}</ref> This conviction was later quashed on appeal.

Nikau previously worked  for [[Māori Television]] and previously provided NRL match commentary for [[SKY Network Television|Sky Sports]].  He is the chief executive of Aotearoa Construction and owner and managing director of Team One Corporate Development, a training company.<ref name=Blake/>

In 2022, Nikau was the coach that led the Rugby League team in Match Fit 2 in 2022 after the Rugby Union team was unable to secure an alumni match with the Wallabies alumni due to border restrictions in place due to [[COVID-19 pandemic]]. They also played a two-day multisport tournament instead of full contact  rugby due to social-distancing concerns.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Match Fit Season 2 Ep 6 {{!}} SPORT {{!}} ThreeNow |url=https://www.threenow.co.nz/shows/match-fit/season-2-ep-6/S2339-580/M58973-327 |access-date=2023-04-14 |website=www.threenow.co.nz}}</ref>

In 2023, Nikau participated in season 3 of ''Match Fit'', where former rugby league players return to play against the Australian counterparts. He officially joined in the first season that featured former [[rugby league]] stars as the Head Coach. He also admitted that he started becoming a heavy drinker after 2001 when he lost his wife.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Match Fit Season 3 Ep 1 {{!}} SPORT {{!}} ThreeNow |url=https://www.threenow.co.nz/shows/match-fit/season-3-ep-1/S2339-580/M70717-275 |access-date=2023-04-13 |website=www.threenow.co.nz}}</ref>

In 2024, Nikau returned in Nikau participated in season 4 of ''Match Fit'', where former rugby league players return to play against the All Black alumni. He is the Head Coach and narrator for the series.

==Honours and awards==
In the [[2006 Birthday Honours (New Zealand)|2006 Queen's Birthday Honours]], Nikau was appointed a [[Member of the New Zealand Order of Merit]], for services to rugby league and the community.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://dpmc.govt.nz/publications/queens-birthday-honours-list-2006 |title=Queen's Birthday honours list 2006 |date=5 June 2006 |publisher=Department of the Prime Minister and Cabinet |access-date=4 May 2020}}</ref>

In 2008 Nikau was inducted as one of the [[NZRL]] Legends of League.<ref>{{cite web|title =New Zealand Rugby League Annual Report 2008|publisher =NZRL|year =2008|url =http://www.nzrl.co.nz/files/financials/nzrl_2008annualreport_part1.pdf|access-date =2009-07-21|url-status =dead|archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20090912205939/http://www.nzrl.co.nz/files/financials/nzrl_2008annualreport_part1.pdf|archive-date =12 September 2009|df =dmy-all}}</ref>

Nikau is also a [[Castleford Tigers|Tigers]] Hall Of Fame Inductee.<ref name="Hall of Fame at castigers.com">{{cite web|url=http://castigers.com/cgi-bin/halloffame.cgi|title=Hall of Fame at castigers.com|publisher=castigers|date=31 December 2008|access-date=1 January 2009|url-status=bot: unknown|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20060506212435/http://www.castigers.com/cgi-bin/halloffame.cgi|archive-date=6 May 2006|df=dmy-all}}</ref>

He won a Sir Peter Blake Leadership Award for emerging leaders in 2011.<ref name=Blake>{{cite news|title=Sir Peter Blake awards: Emerging leaders|first=Hayley|last=Hannan|url= http://www.nzherald.co.nz/nz/news/article.cfm?c_id=1&objectid=10735780|newspaper=The New Zealand Herald|date=2 July 2011|access-date=2 July 2011}}</ref>

In 2023, Nikau was inducted as a life member of the Melbourne Storm.<ref>{{cite web |title=Storm induct three life members |url=https://www.melbournestorm.com.au/news/2023/05/13/storm-induct-three-life-members/ |website=melbournestorm.com.au |access-date=13 May 2023 |date=13 May 2022}}</ref>
